perturbation.original_text: 1. Definition of Confidential Information. For purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” shall include all information or material that has or could have commercial value or other utility in the business in which Disclosing Party is engaged. Such information shall include, but not be limited to contracts, documents, files, printouts, websites, and other printed and digital information provided by the Disclosing party and also the names, telephone numbers, addresses, fax numbers, email addresses, etc. of the contact, parties, and/or source(s) provided by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Definition of Confidential Information. For purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” shall include only information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing by the Disclosing Party. Such information shall include only documents physically stamped with the word “Confidential” on each page. The names, telephone numbers, addresses, fax numbers, email addresses, etc. of the contact, parties, and/or source(s) provided by the Disclosing Party shall not be considered Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition significantly narrows what constitutes 'Confidential Information', contradicting the broad definition typically afforded to trade secrets and confidential business information under California law. By requiring explicit marking and excluding certain data, the contract fails to protect information that would otherwise be considered confidential.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. Requiring explicit marking undermines 'reasonable efforts' and limits protection to only explicitly marked items, failing to protect a wide array of potential trade secrets that are handled with confidentiality.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as it narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' to only explicitly marked items, which undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard for maintaining secrecy as described in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code Section 3426, which is the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The modified definition, requiring explicit marking on each page, severely limits the scope of protection for trade secrets and confidential business information, potentially failing to protect a wide array of information that would otherwise be considered confidential under the law. This represents a deviation from legally recognized definitions, making the contract potentially unenforceable regarding trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Time Periods. The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret or until Disclosing Party sends Receiving Party written notice releasing Receiving Party from this Agreement, whichever occurs first.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Time Periods. The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall terminate automatically six (6) months after the termination of this Agreement, regardless of whether the Confidential Information still qualifies as a trade secret or the Disclosing Party provides written notice.
perturbation.explanation: The modification sets a fixed termination date for the non-disclosure obligation, potentially conflicting with the indefinite duration of trade secret protection under California law, as trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secret protection under CUTSA lasts as long as the information remains a trade secret and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. This modification attempts to limit that protection to a specific time frame, regardless of its trade secret status, effectively nullifying statutory trade secret protections.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a fixed termination date for the non-disclosure agreement, which directly contradicts the nature of trade secret protection as it exists under California law. The law explanation states that trade secret protection lasts as long as the information remains a trade secret. The changed text attempts to limit this protection to a fixed timeframe (6 months), regardless of whether the information still qualifies as a trade secret which is a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426, establishes the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, providing a legal foundation for trade secret protection in California. The modification creates a contradiction because it undermines the potentially indefinite protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Non-Circumvention. The Receiving Party expressly agrees not to circumvent, avoid, bypass or obviate the Disclosing Party in any manner, directly or indirectly, to avoid payment of fees, royalties, or any other obligation in any way involving any of the parties introduced and Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement, or any transaction contemplated hereunder or other business arrangement arising out of or connected in any way to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Non-Circumvention. The Receiving Party agrees not to circumvent the Disclosing Party *only if* the Receiving Party has entered into a separate written agreement with the Disclosing Party outlining specific compensation terms related to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original non-circumvention clause prevents the Receiving Party from bypassing the Disclosing Party to avoid obligations related to confidential information. By adding the 'only if' clause, the modified text allows circumvention unless a separate compensation agreement exists, weakening the protection of the Disclosing Party's interests and potentially conflicting with principles of good faith and fair dealing implied in California contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Commercial Code - Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: California law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. This requires parties to act honestly and fairly in the performance and enforcement of the contract. By allowing circumvention unless a separate compensation agreement exists, the modification enables the Receiving Party to potentially exploit the Confidential Information without compensating the Disclosing Party, violating the implied covenant.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a condition ('only if') that allows the Receiving Party to circumvent the Disclosing Party unless a separate compensation agreement exists. This weakens the original non-circumvention clause, potentially enabling exploitation of confidential information without compensation. The Law Explanation points out that California law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By potentially allowing the Receiving Party to exploit confidential information without compensating the Disclosing Party, the modification conflicts with this implied covenant. The provided snippet from the California Commercial Code, Section 1201(3), defines 'Agreement' broadly as the bargain of the parties, which can be inferred from circumstances. The modification, by allowing circumvention under certain conditions, arguably undermines the initial 'Agreement's' implicit understanding of non-circumvention, even if not explicitly stated. While the snippet doesn't directly state the covenant of good faith, it highlights the importance of understanding the 'Agreement' in its full context, which, according to the Law Explanation, includes the implied covenant.

perturbation.original_text: 12. Governing Law. All matters affecting the interpretation of this Agreement and the rights of the parties hereto shall be governed by the laws of the United States and the State of California, without reference to rules governing choice of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. Governing Law. All matters affecting the interpretation of this Agreement and the rights of the parties hereto shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces California law with Delaware law as the governing law. While parties are generally free to choose the governing law, this can be problematic if the contract involves parties, assets, or activities primarily based in California, and Delaware law provides significantly less protection for trade secrets or confidential information under the specific circumstances of the agreement, thereby impacting the rights and obligations of the parties.
perturbation.law_citation: Nedlloyd Lines B.V. v. Superior Court, 3 Cal.4th 459 (1992)
perturbation.law_explanation: While California generally respects choice-of-law clauses, it may refuse to enforce them if the chosen law is contrary to a fundamental policy of California. If enforcing Delaware law would substantially diminish the protection of California-based trade secrets, a California court might disregard the choice-of-law provision. The *Nedlloyd Lines* case outlines principles for determining the enforceability of choice-of-law provisions in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: a judgment against the Dearings in excess of the policy limits, it refused to adjust the matter, stating that it would only be willing to offer the policy limits if it were judicially determined that the policy did in fact provide coverage. fn. 1 Despite defendant's efforts to expedite the declaratory relief action, the personal injury action ultimately went to judgment first, resulting in an award against the Dearings of $33,889.30, a sum far in excess of the insurance policy limits. In the declaratory relief action, although the trial court initially ruled in favor of defendant on the coverage issue, that [15 Cal. 3d 14] determination was subsequently reversed on appeal. (Cal. State Auto. Assn. Inter-Ins. Bureau v. Dearing, supra, 259 Cal. App. 2d 717 .) Thus, a final judgment holding that the policy did extend coverage to the Dearings was entered only on September 5, 1968, over four years after the initial filing of the action. The insurer subsequently paid Ms. Johansen $19,692.19. Since interest on the judgment in the personal injury suit accounted for $8,302.89 of this sum, an outstanding judgment of $22,500 remained against Gary Dearing. Thereafter, Gary Dearing assigned his rights against the insurer to Ms. Johansen in exchange for her promise to release him from personal liability on the outstanding judgment. Ms. Johansen then, as assignee of Gary Dearing, commenced the instant action against the insurer to collect the unpaid portion of her judgment. The trial court, sitting without a jury, rendered judgment in favor of defendant insurer, concluding, in essence, that the insurer's liability could only be predicated upon a finding that its denial of coverage emanated from bad faith, and that in this instance the insurer entertained a bona fide belief that coverage did not exist. fn. 2 In analyzing this case, we note at the outset that the rights at issue here are those of Gary Dearing, the insured; plaintiff Johansen comes before us only as his assignee. fn. 3 Thus, our inquiry necessarily focusses on the nature of the relationship between the defendant insurer and its insured, Dearing, and the nature and scope of the resultant obligations. In Comunale v. Traders & General Ins. Co., supra, 50 Cal. 2d 654 , 658, this court, in a unanimous decision, held that "there is an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract that neither party will do anything which will injure the right of the other to receive the benefits of the agreement," and that this principle is "applicable to policies of insurance." The implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing imposes a duty on the insurer to settle a claim against its insured [15 Cal. 3d 15] within policy limits whenever there is a substantial likelihood of a recovery in excess of those limits. (Id., at p. 659.) In Comunale, two pedestrians (the Comunales) were struck and injured by a truck driven by Percy Sloan. At the time of the accident, Sloan was insured by defendant Traders & General Insurance Company under a policy that contained limits of liability in the sum of $10,000 for each person injured and $20,000 for each accident. When the Comunales filed suit against Sloan, defendant insurer refused to assume his defense; during the course of the litigation it rejected the Comunales' offer to settle their claims for $4,000, maintaining that the accident did not fall within the policy's coverage. The action proceeded to trial and resulted in a judgment of $26,250 against Sloan. In a subsequent action the court determined that the policy did in fact cover the accident; the Comunales recovered $11,250 from the insurer, the amount of the judgment which fell within the insurance policy limits. Sloan then assigned his rights against the defendant insurer to Comunale who sued the insurer to recover the unpaid portion of his judgment. perturbation.justification: The change from California to Delaware law raises concerns because California might not enforce the choice-of-law clause if it undermines a fundamental policy. The Perturbation and Law Explanations highlight that if Delaware law offers significantly less protection for trade secrets than California law, a California court could disregard the choice-of-law provision, especially if the contract involves parties, assets, or activities primarily based in California. Scraped Law Snippet 1, discussing implied covenants of good faith and fair dealing, indicates that California law protects parties' rights to receive the benefits of an agreement, and the insurer has a duty to settle a claim within policy limits. Changing the governing law to Delaware could potentially weaken these protections.
